summary: On-call responsibility is a two-way compact: engineers should own their code in production, but management must ensure on-call doesn't suck by investing in tooling, tracking alerts, protecting on-call time from project work, and treating every out-of-hours page as urgent. Managers who fail to hold up their end of this bargain deserve to lose their engineers.
key_points: On-call is a handshake between engineering and management — engineers own their code, managers ensure the experience is sustainable
Engineers on call should be freed from normal project work to focus on fixes, tooling, and guardrails
Every out-of-hours alert should be treated seriously and the underlying cause fixed, even if it slows product development
Reliability work and technical debt deserve equal standing with product work on the roadmap
It's possible to make on-call fully opt-in, a source of pride, and something engineers genuinely look forward to
money_quotes: It is engineering's responsibility to be on call and own their code. It is management's responsibility to make sure that on call does not suck. This is a handshake, it goes both ways, and if you do not hold up your end they should quit and leave you.
Tossing it off to ops after tests pass is nothing but a thinly veiled form of engineering classism, and you can't build high-performing systems by breaking up your feedback loops this way.
Night time pages are heart attacks, not diabetes.
Any asshole can write some code; owning and tending complex systems for the long run is the hard part.
Your greatest enemy is how easily you accept the status quo, and then make up excuses for why it is necessarily this way.
key_insight: On-call is a mutual compact where management owes engineers sustainable conditions — protected time, good tooling, alert tracking, and treating every page as urgent — and failing this obligation justifies engineers walking out the door.
